creativeNotes: This is a strong episode that earns its central premise: repair as a repeated practice, not a one-time transaction. The structure is elegant — the branching isn't cosmetic, it actually tracks emotional consistency (chase vs. avoid, own it vs. deflect, hold the line vs. hedge) and the outcomes genuinely diverge in tone rather than just wording. Alex's boundary-setting scene (choice-3) is the standout beat in the whole piece — 'not even the safe version' is exactly the kind of specific, hard-won line that makes a middle-schooler's trauma feel real without over-explaining it. Mr. Alvarez's engineered pairing is a nice structural device that keeps the adult purposeful rather than incidental. The four-tap tic paying off differently across branches (four vs. two taps) is a lovely, subtle piece of craft.

story: The escalation from cafeteria joke to library boundary to public presentation moment is well-paced and each scene raises stakes logically — no note needed structurally.

Consider a touch more texture in the 'lonely win' ending — right now Jordan's noticing the empty seat is doing a lot of work alone; one more beat of Wren actively choosing not to act would sharpen the 'cost of ease' theme.

The fractured/broken path resolves quickly (scene-6-showdown-fractured into scene-7-resolution-broken) — it lands, but a single added line of Wren's internal rationalization before the freeze would make the abandonment feel more like a choice than a reflex.
character: Alex is the strongest-written character here — guarded, precise, funny in short bursts ('I hate that I laughed' energy even without that exact line appearing). The choice to have them ask 'Why should I trust you with anything else?' as a literal, non-rhetorical question is excellent characterization.

Maya's 'Please tell me you're not actually presenting the doodle kingdom' repeating verbatim across three branch variants is a smart structural choice — it isolates Wren's response as the only variable, which is exactly what the theme demands.
Jordan's conflict-avoidance is consistent with his established profile ('no for real, that'll be fine, probably') — nice continuity of his established flaw without making him unlikeable.
dialogue: Wren's apology language in the 'good' branch ('I took something you showed me — in confidence — and turned it into a bit') is a little more articulate than typical panicked-13-year-old speech, but it's plausible for Wren specifically given her established self-awareness, so it works.
Internal lines like 'Not nothing. Not enough, either' are doing efficient emotional work without over-explaining — good restraint.

Minor: s6s-3's stage direction 'Alex is close enough to hear. Doesn't move. Waits.' is repeated almost identically across all three showdown variants — fine for a template but worth varying slightly in final pass so it doesn't read as copy-paste in sequence.
tone: Tone stays grounded and avoids after-school-special moralizing — nobody delivers a speech about kindness, the lesson is entirely enacted through behavior, which fits the world's 'grounded realism' mandate.
The humor (Sir Fluffington the Third, Jordan's gum stockpile callback energy) keeps the episode from feeling like a PSA despite the heavy theme — good balance.

inspirationReferences.title: PEN15
inspirationReferences.reasoning: Similar commitment to letting a small social betrayal carry real emotional weight without adult-voiced moralizing, and to specific, non-generic embarrassment (the invented kingdom mirrors PEN15's use of hyper-specific kid artifacts as emotional shorthand).

inspirationReferences.title: Ghost Boys / any Jason Reynolds middle-grade work
inspirationReferences.reasoning: Reynolds' dialogue often trusts kids to be precise and articulate about pain without sounding adult — the model for Alex's boundary-setting line.
